---
title: "A Rube Goldberg Pipeline for Sharing Links"
description: "How a single iOS share-sheet tap traverses half a dozen systems (Pythonista, GitHub, Quarto, Mastodon) to land a blog post and a toot."
author: synesis
date: "2026-04-20"
categories: [coding, automation]
image: cover.jpg
---

![Rube Goldberg's *Self-Operating Napkin*, the canonical absurd contraption (Professor Butts, 1931). Image via [Wikimedia Commons (public domain)](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Self-operating_napkin_(Rube_Goldberg_cartoon_with_caption).jpg).](cover.jpg){fig-align="center"}

Most posts here start as a link or a paragraph I read on my phone, in Mastodon or RSS or an article. By the time I'm back at my Mac, the thread is usually gone: the link buried under three days of tabs, the half-formed reaction long since dissolved.

What I wanted, in three steps:

1. **I capture on my phone** in seconds, with whatever sliver of a thought I have at the moment.
2. **I finish on my Mac** at leisure, with VSCode and Claude Code.
3. **I publish to the blog AND cross-post to Mastodon** in one motion, preview cards included.

What it took: half a dozen different systems chained together. It starts when a scaffolded draft lands in my repo within seconds of a phone tap. Hours or days later, when I'm back at my Mac and have written my commentary, running the `publish` command takes about three minutes to put the post live on `benjaminhan.net` and a toot out on `sigmoid.social`. The systems involved are the iOS share sheet, a Pythonista script, GitHub's Contents API, GitHub Actions, GitHub Pages, and Mastodon's preview-card service.

The result is more engineering than bookmarking really needs. Below is the contraption, and the spots where it tends to stick.

## The pipeline

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
    subgraph capture [" "]
        direction LR
        A[iOS Share Sheet] --> B[Pythonista]
        B -->|GitHub Contents API| C[draft .qmd in repo]
        C -->|git pull + /expand-draft| D[Expanded draft]
        D --> M[Manual edit<br/>VSCode + Claude Code]
    end
    subgraph deploy [" "]
        direction LR
        E[git mv to posts/] -->|git push| F[GitHub Actions]
        F -->|Quarto build| G[Static site deploy]
        G -->|wait for CI green| H[POST to Mastodon]
        H --> I[Toot with link card]
    end
    M -->|/publish-draft| E
    style capture fill:none,stroke:none
    style deploy fill:none,stroke:none
```

Each arrow is someone else's HTTP request, someone else's cache, someone else's auth scheme.

## What each stage actually does

- **iOS Pythonista** captures the URL plus a handful of prompts (title, slug, optional one-liner, "Notes to Claude," "is the post mine?", "cross-post to Mastodon?"), auto-detects the source platform (Mastodon, YouTube, arxiv, Substack, GitHub, etc.) from the URL host, and `PUT`s a stub `.qmd` to the repo via GitHub's Contents API. The platform tag tells the next stage how to dispatch.
- **`/expand-draft` skill** runs on the Mac in Claude Code. It reads the stub's `source-url` and `source-platform`, fetches the page, extracts `og:image` / `og:description`, suggests categories from the existing tag set, downloads the cover image (with a citation in the figcaption), and writes a STYLE.md-compliant draft. If `claude-notes` is set, the skill follows it as per-draft direction. If `source-mine: true`, it skips the framing-commentary scaffolding.
- **Manual editing** happens between expand and publish. I open the draft in VSCode and write or polish my commentary with Claude Code at hand. Quick link shares take minutes. Essays can take days. The expanded scaffolding stays deliberately minimal.
- **`/publish-draft` skill** validates the draft against the style guide, strips scaffolding, `git mv`s to `posts/`, commits, pushes.
- **GitHub Actions** invokes Quarto to render the site. Quarto then runs three `post-render` scripts in sequence: `feed-render.py` rasterizes Mermaid diagrams and MathJax expressions to SVG so the RSS feed carries real images, `feed-utm.py` injects UTM parameters into outbound links for source attribution, and `feed-paginate.py` slices the feed into RFC 5005 paged archives so the index file stays small while subscribers can still walk back through history.
- **Mastodon cross-post** (only if `also-toot: true`) waits for CI to finish, verifies the URL is reachable, composes a ≤500-character status from the post's title and category-derived hashtags, asks me to confirm, then `POST`s to `/api/v1/statuses`. The token lives in macOS Keychain and never appears in any committed file.

## Gotchas

The pipeline above looks clean in a flowchart. It is not. Here are the bumps I hit, in roughly the order they showed up.

- **`dialogs.input_alert` doesn't block in Pythonista's share extension.** The dialog works fine when I tap ▶ inside the Pythonista app. From the share sheet, it races through every prompt with empty inputs. Symptom: nothing happens, no error, the script finishes silently. Fix: switch to `console.input_alert`. Same signature, behaves correctly in the extension sandbox.
- **GitHub Contents API needs `sha` to update an existing file.** I hit this on a slug collision (HTTP 422, "sha wasn't supplied"). The script now `GET`s the path first. If the file exists, it offers Replace (passing the existing `sha` so the `PUT` is treated as an update) or Cancel.
- **Mastodon caches link-preview failures aggressively.** I posted a status with a URL that was still 404 because CI hadn't deployed yet. Mastodon fetched, failed, and cached the miss for hours. The blog post eventually goes live but the toot stays card-less. Fix: the publish skill now does `gh run watch` until CI is green and `curl -sI` confirms 200 before composing the toot.
- **Quarto's `draft-mode: gone` strips draft pages to empty HTML.** Local `quarto preview` shows blank pages for files in `drafts/`. Workaround: a `_quarto-preview.yml` profile that flips `draft-mode: unlinked` for previews. Production CI builds without the `--profile=preview` flag, so the deployed site never includes drafts.
- **VSCode's Quarto extension doesn't inherit `terminal.integrated.env.osx`.** Setting `QUARTO_PROFILE=preview` in workspace settings only affects the integrated terminal. The extension spawns Quarto in a separate process tree with its own inherited env. Real fix: `export QUARTO_PROFILE=preview` in `~/.zshrc`, then `Cmd-Q` and relaunch VSCode from the terminal so the extension inherits it.
- **`image:` frontmatter is metadata, not body content.** Quarto uses `image:` only for `og:image` meta tags and listing thumbnails. It does not render an inline cover image on the post page itself. The body needs an explicit `![](cover.jpg){fig-align="center"}` for the cover to actually appear.
- **`.claude/` was gitignored, hiding the per-project skills.** The first commit of `expand-draft.md` and `publish-draft.md` resulted in zero tracked files. The directory was excluded entirely. Git's rule is that a re-include cannot reach a file under an already-excluded parent directory, so `!.claude/skills/` does not work against `.claude/`. The fix is to widen the exclusion to `.claude/*` first, then re-include `.claude/skills/`.

## Why bother

Three reasons:

- **The iOS-to-Mac handoff matches how I actually find good links.** On a phone, in an RSS or Mastodon reader, away from a keyboard. Capturing them as scaffolded drafts means I don't lose the thread between "I should put more thought to it" and "I'm back at the Mac." The drafts queue up. I work on them when I have time.
- **The cross-post closes the loop.** Mastodon followers see a properly formatted toot with a preview card. The blog gets the post. The RSS feed and `llms.txt` get the entry. One iOS tap sets all three in motion, and the HTTP hops underneath are hidden by the time I push the button, which is the part the system is supposed to handle.
- **The pipeline keeps a human in the loop.** The phone capture is mine. The commentary is mine. The publish command is a manual step, not a webhook. The Mastodon toot waits for my confirmation before going out. Automation handles plumbing. Editorial calls stay with me.

The pipeline is elaborate, fragile, and a little excessive. For a workflow that runs every few days, that is also fine.
